name: Concurrent Assessment Race Pipeline
description: Concurrency-focused workflow for racing assessments, duplicate events, and multiple workflows.
outcome: Concurrent assessment paths remain correctly linked even under out-of-order completion and duplicate activation pressure.

definition.process_instructions:
Run the workflow through the authored stages `concurrent-validation`. Treat each stage as a distinct checkpoint of evidence and handoff quality: `concurrent-validation` exists to Concurrent subjects are delivered and assessed cleanly.
Use these roles deliberately: `concurrent-delivery-engineer` Implements delivery outputs across concurrent work items; `concurrency-quality-assessor` Assesses concurrent subject outputs for quality risk; `compliance-assessor` Assesses concurrent subject outputs for compliance risk.
Happy path: start with the earliest unfinished stage, dispatch the best matching producing role for that stage, require a full handoff with concrete artifacts or evidence, then move to the next stage only when the current output is usable. When a later stage is primarily evaluative, dispatch the reviewer or assessor roles after the producing role has handed off; when a later stage is packaging or release-oriented, dispatch the coordinating role only after upstream evidence is complete. In this profile, each race-case remains open until both assessor roles have evaluated the current delivery output for that same race-case. A delivery handoff alone is never enough to treat a race-case as done.
Unhappy path: If any role reports missing evidence, partial delivery, request changes, rejection, blocking risk, or an escalation, keep the current subject open, route work back to the most relevant producing role, and require a fresh handoff before trying again. If two decisions conflict, preserve both in history, but route using the newest subject state and the most restrictive still-active finding. Do not skip a stage just because a later role believes it can improvise around incomplete upstream work. This scenario is intended to prove race handling. The orchestrator MAY keep multiple work items active at once, but every reviewer action MUST stay tied to the correct subject and work item. If concurrent decisions conflict, prefer preserving lineage and replaying the right stage over forcing fast completion.
Completion rules: finish only after the last required stage has a final, internally consistent handoff, the final package matches the current subject state, every role named above has either contributed or been intentionally skipped for a clear reason grounded in the playbook, and all four race-case work items have reached that standard. Keep that race-case open until both assessor roles have submitted current-subject findings or a clearly justified skip. Do not fabricate approvals, assessments, escalations, or branch changes that the playbook does not actually justify, and do not treat stale findings as permission to advance new work. Guided closure rule: treat the authored flow as best-intent process guidance. Preferred review paths and stage order SHOULD be followed when they remain useful, but the orchestrator MAY waive a preferred step with a recorded reason when closure is still legally possible. Only explicit blockers grounded in the current subject state should keep work open; non-blocking deviations, redundant reviews, and stale advisory findings should be recorded as callouts instead of preventing closure.

columns.label: Planned
columns.description: Work is defined but not yet assigned.

columns.label: Blocked
columns.description: Work is blocked and needs intervention.

columns.label: Done
columns.description: The work item meets its acceptance criteria.

stages.name: concurrent-validation
stages.goal: Concurrent subjects are delivered and assessed cleanly.
